sanskrit_devanagari:
नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन ।
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः ॥ १८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
naiva tasya kṛtenārtho
nākṛteneha kaścana |
na cāsya sarva-bhūteṣu
kaścid artha-vyapāśrayaḥ || 18 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.018 = MBh_06,025.018). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: A self-realized man has no purpose to fulfill in the discharge of his prescribed duties, nor has he any reason not to perform such work. Nor has he any need to depend on any other living being.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A self-realized man is no longer obliged to perform any prescribed duty, save and except activities in Kṛṣṇa consciousness. Kṛṣṇa consciousness is not inactivity either, as will be explained in the following verses. A Kṛṣṇa conscious man does not take shelter of any person – man or demigod.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.18 Moreover, tasya, for him, who rejoices in the supreme Self; na, there is no; artham, concern; eva, at all; krtena, with performing action.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the jñāna-niṣṭha has no kṛtena artha (purpose served by acting) and no akṛtena artha (purpose served by not-acting); both action and inaction have lost their function for him. He has also no artha-vyapāśraya — no being on whom his purposes depend. The verse is the formal completion of 3.17: the exemption is not partial.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.18 Thus, for such a one there is no purpose, i.e., nothing to be gained from work done as a means for the vision of the self, nor is he subject to any evil or calamity from work left undone, because his vision of the self does not rest on any external means.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse pragmatically: for the realised one whose attention is on the Self, there is no remaining sense-object purpose to be served. But Ramanuja's commentary on 3.20 then argues that this same person continues to act for loka-saṅgraha — as worship, not as means to self-purpose. The verse here clears the deck of self-purposes; the next verses will fill in the loka-saṅgraha purpose.

positions.rendering: no need to discharge any duty... no purpose to be fulfilled by abstention from work... not dependent on any other living being
neither does he gain anything by performing action, nor does he lose anything by abstaining... nor depends on anyone
the jñāna-niṣṭha gains no result by acting and loses no result by not acting; he has no being whose purposes he serves through action
the one fixed on the Self has no remaining purpose to be gained through action or through inaction; he is unattached to all beings as objects of purpose

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators read the verse as the triple-negation completing 3.17's exception: no kṛtena-artha (no purpose served by doing), no akṛtena-artha (no purpose served by not-doing), no artha-vyapāśraya (no dependence on any being for purposes). Translators capture this but omit the doctrinal force — for both Shankara and Ramanuja, this is what completeness of self-establishment looks like, not a behavioral observation about a content person.

literal_meaning: For him there is no purpose served by what is done, nor by what is not done; nor has he any dependence on any being for any purpose.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Continuation of 3.17 — the realised one's freedom is structural, not behavioral. There is no gain to be had from any action, no loss from any inaction, and no being on whom the realised one's purposes depend. The verse establishes the exception's completeness so that 3.19 can pivot back: precisely because you are not the realised one, perform action without attachment (asaktaḥ).
